2606.00672.title: Medication-Aware Financial Exploitation Detection for Alzheimer's Patients Using Edge-Aware Interaction Risk Modeling
2606.00672.paper_summary: Financial exploitation is a growing concern for people with Alzheimer's disease, especially during periods of reduced cognitive stability. Conventional fraud detection systems usually rely on financial behavior alone and ignore clinically relevant factors that may alter vulnerability. This paper proposes a medication-aware framework that synchronizes medication adherence with transaction-level monitoring to improve detection of cognitively risky financial events. A hybrid simulation dataset was constructed for 180 patients across 45 days, producing 8,100 medication records and 30,855 transactions. The framework evaluates amount anomaly, vendor novelty, transaction frequency, time deviation, and medication adherence through financial-only, additive medication-aware, and interaction-aware logistic models. Results show that the financial-only baseline obtained the highest global F1-score of 0.5000, but the interaction-aware model improved recall during medication-induced vulnerability windows from 0.7442 to 0.9070 and achieved the highest average precision for ranked high-risk cases. The findings suggest that medication adherence is most useful as a contextual modifier of financial risk rather than as an isolated predictor.
2606.00672.paper_summary_zh: 財務剝削對於阿茲海默症患者來說是一個日益關注的問題，特別是在認知穩定性降低的時期。傳統的詐騙檢測系統通常僅依賴財務行為，忽略了可能改變脆弱性的臨床相關因素。本文提出了一個藥物認知框架，將藥物遵從性與交易層級的監控同步，以改善對認知風險財務事件的檢測。為180名患者在45天內構建了一個混合模擬數據集，產生了8,100條藥物記錄和30,855筆交易。該框架通過僅基於財務的、附加藥物認知的和互動認知的邏輯模型來評估金額異常、供應商新穎性、交易頻率、時間偏差和藥物遵從性。結果顯示，僅基於財務的基準獲得了最高的全球F1分數0.5000，但互動認知模型在藥物誘導的脆弱性窗口期間將召回率從0.7442提高到0.9070，並在排名高風險案例中達到了最高的平均精確度。研究結果表明，藥物遵從性作為財務風險的上下文修飾因素最為有用，而不是作為孤立的預測因子。
2606.00672.author: Farzana Akter et.al.
2606.00672.authors: Farzana Akter, Lisan Al Amin, Rakib Hossain, Chaitanya Gunupudi, Faisal Quader
